step.title: Step 9
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT from (29,28) to escape dead end')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, DOWN, RIGHT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 78, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Each action moves player 6 pixels. The 6×6 coarse grid has a clear BFS path of 9 moves from current position (0,1) to goal (5,5).
- Plan: Test all 4 directions from (28,29) to map actual connectivity, then build correct graph.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 77 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 4.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting results:
- (28, 29) UP → (21, 28)
- (21, 28) DOWN → (29, 28)
- (29, 28) RIGHT → (29, 28) (NO CHANGE!)
- (29, 28) DOWN → (29, 28) (NO CHANGE!)
So from (29, 28), RIGHT and DOWN don't work. The player is stuck! This means (29, 28) is a dead end for RIGHT and DOWN directions.
Let me check what's around (29, 28):
- The player is at (29, 28)
- RIGHT doesn't work, so there's probably a wall to the right
- DOWN doesn't work, so there's probably a wall below
Now I'm at (29, 28). I need to get back and try a different route. Let me try LEFT and UP from here.
From the data I have:
- (28, 21) UP → (21, 22)
- (28, 21) RIGHT → (28, 29)
- (28, 29) LEFT → (28, 21)
- (28, 29) UP → (21, 28)
- (21, 28) DOWN → (29, 28)
- (29, 28) RIGHT → (29, 28) (stuck)
- (29, 28) DOWN → (29, 28) (stuck)
So from (29, 28), I need to try LEFT or UP to get out.
Let me try LEFT from (29, 28). This should go back to (28, 21) or somewhere nearby.
Actually, wait. Let me think about the movement pattern more carefully.
From (28, 21):
- UP → (21, 22): moved 7 rows up, 1 col right
- RIGHT → (28, 29): moved 8 cols right
From (28, 29):
- LEFT → (28, 21): moved 8 cols left
- UP → (21, 28): moved 7 rows up, -1 col
From (21, 28):
- DOWN → (29, 28): moved 8 rows down
So the pattern seems to be: the player moves to the farthest reachable position in the direction pressed, but constrained to the connected walkable area.
From (28, 21), pressing UP goes to (21, 22), which is the farthest position upward in the connected area.
From (28, 21), pressing RIGHT goes to (28, 29), which is the farthest position rightward.
From (28, 29), pressing UP goes to (21, 28), which is the farthest position upward from there.
From (21, 28), pressing DOWN goes to (29, 28), which is the farthest position downward.
From (29, 28), pressing RIGHT doesn't work because there's no walkable area to the right.
From (29, 28), pressing DOWN doesn't work because there's no walkable area below.
So the movement rule is: press a direction, and the player slides to the farthest reachable position in that direction within the connected walkable area.
Now I need to find a path from (29, 28) to the goal at (46, 46). Let me first get out of this dead end by pressing LEFT or UP.
Let me try LEFT from (29, 28).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model revision: Movement = slide to farthest reachable position in direction pressed. From (29,28), RIGHT and DOWN are blocked (dead end). Need to escape via LEFT or UP.
Plan: Try LEFT from (29,28) to get out, then map connectivity toward goal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Currently at (29, 28). Try LEFT to escape.
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT from (29,28) to escape dead end')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
pos = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        pos = tuple(n['boundary'][0])
        break
print(f"(29,28) LEFT -> {pos}")

# Now try UP from there
predict(expect_change=True, note='UP from new position')
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
pos2 = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        pos2 = tuple(n['boundary'][0])
        break
print(f"{pos} UP -> {pos2}")

# Now try RIGHT from there
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT from new position')
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
pos3 = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        pos3 = tuple(n['boundary'][0])
        break
print(f"{pos2} RIGHT -> {pos3}")

# Now try DOWN from there (toward goal)
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN from new position')
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
pos4 = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        pos4 = tuple(n['boundary'][0])
        break
print(f"{pos3} DOWN -> {pos4}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
